commentBody: Spurs went on quite  a run as I was doing this.   Impressive team. Hope they win it.
I'm so glad Zhou and Mallory decided to give it another try! Congrats and well done!

Delightful! In addition to the themed articles of clothing, we got to see an AO DAI, a MINISKIRT, and a TUXEDO. To me, this was a perfect Wednesday puzzle. 

N.B. Yesterday, we had a civilian take on TAKE POINT. Today we have only metaphoric BATTLE SCARS. Are the editors suggesting we should gird our loins?
Other than the cross of MAE and AODAI, neither of which would necessarily be common or guessable, this was a good puzzle.

End piece? Could be omit just as easily as OBIT.  You omit the piece from the final product. 

Ordered. Could be made just as easily as BADE. You order a product and have it made. 

I dislike when there are plausible solutions to clues. Tell me why the above doesn’t work. I could be missing something.

Three separate thoughts:

The clue for 20A should be Ɉimi⅃, not timiL, but I suppose there would have been Unicode difficulties if they used that.

It's interesting how after my commenting on clues for the entry LOO two days ago, in which I included [John for Elton John], and then in the Wed. puzzle, CLEESE comes up in the puzzle when I had just also referenced the clue [John for Cleese], today we have practically the opposite, [John from Britain] for ELTON.

Finally, I'd like to point out that this puzzle contains elements of visual wordplay not unlike the H-O-H strings last Thursday, insomuch as playing around with letters in some unorthodox way suggests something else.  However, the author has not chosen (correctly so!) not to call this puzzle by the R-word.  All is right with the world.

This puzzle PIQUEd my interest.  I was able to solve it without understanding the themers, and got a good laugh out of figuring them out after the fact.  For some reason I have a fondness for words with odd combinations of vowels, so GOOIER and AODAI (previously unknown to me) were catnip.  I also have to confess that I have used EXACTAMUNDO myself, and got a chuckle out of seeing it here.  Getting it fairly quickly greatly aided my solve.  Finally,  I appreciated the disguised clue for old standby OREO.
I really like this theme of type.
Quick bit of fun after watching the Brewers beat the North-Siders at American Family Field South and before bed. Thanks.

So we can all agree that this is a rebus puzzle, right?

Right???

Let the fighting begin...

Was like smooth peanut butter on warm toast. Very well done! 

What’s your favorite toast?

This was fun. Good theme! Good clues!

My only actual error was llm for AIS -- I hadn't been sure if the spitting animal was LLAMA or camel, so left that blank, and didn't know AODAI, so 13D was fully blank on my first pass through downs. I prefer calling ChatGPT a Large Language Model, because AI as a term can refer to so many things, and ChatGPT doesn't really have intelligence per se.

Clever, cute, ton of fun. 

As a golfer however, what in the world is a cut off tee?

Fun, fresh theme. 

The clue for MINI SKIRT showed up in a regular sized font on my app (just it's position was slightly offset)- I'm assuming it's an app bug and it was supposed to be in a much smaller font to fit in with the rest of the theme.

Was the miniskirt clue supposed to be small or otherwise cleverly written? On the Android app, it showed up normally. I needed to use the crossers to solve because I didn't realize it was a theme entry until I had the full answer.

Otherwise great puzzle! Very fun.
Enjoyed 'exactamundo' and 'ao dai' but thought the overall puzz a bit too quick and easy (for Thursday.)

Lately it feels like the trivia and natics are on steroids.

Ok theme at best but not very fun, boring fill, sad Thursday, not much going on here for me.

Usually my favorite day. Couldn't be bothered to look up all the obscure or niche names. 

No the crosses don't help when they are also obscure words or names. Great if it was easy for you. 

Just preempting the unhelpful responses I see when others posts similar experiences.

Maybe we'll get something better next Thursday.

63 Across and 53 Down could have crossed on the letter “M”.  “End Piece” could have been OMIT as in “to remove a piece from publication”.  Likewise,  “Ordered” could have been  MADE. 

Of course OBIT and BADE work , too.

“Central Meeting Points” are NODES?  That’s awful, especially in a corner with a lot of pretty esoteric knowledge-based answers.  The rest of the puzzle great and a lot of fun (if a bit easy) but that upper-right corner ruined it for me.

Somewhere, once, when someone really went out of their way to annoy it, a KOALA got angry enough to spit.
Or so I thought for a while there.

A bit easy for a Thursday, but a fun exercise. A few semi-stumpers and nice misdirection here and there, but it came together nicely.

Having done many "wacky wordies" when subscribing to "Games" magazine years ago, it didn't stretch my brain too far to figure out the theme clues and come up with the answers (along with the occasional cross).

Thanks, Zhou and Mallory!

It was a relatively mild Thursday and I I should have finished about 20-25 mins earlier but that CUTOFFTEE/STET cross had me in a chokehold. It took actual chunks of my brain with no hope regeneration..  

No lookups today, but checked to see if my words were right, especially when the music refused to play. Does that count as lookups? In my head, lookups are for finding the answers, while checkups are to your answer is wrong. Let me know.

It was a relatively mild Thursday and I I should have finished about 20-25 mins earlier but that CUTOFFTEE/STET cross had me in a chokehold. It took actual chunks of my brain with no hope regeneration..  

No lookups today, but checked to see if my words were right, especially when the music refused to play. Does that count as lookups? In my head, lookups are for finding the answers, while checkups are to your answer is wrong. 
Let me know in the comments! Like and subscribe, etc 😀
(Do get in touch about 👆🏾 though :)
This was an absolute romp!  One third my average Thursday time.  Didn’t know aodai but guessed the fairly obvious cross….  Rebus’ were meh for me…

Well that was satisfying. Just the right amount of brain crunch and gimmes to make it fun. Even the ones ive never heard of i could get from the crosses. Set a new personal best for Wednesday. You could almost switch yesterday's for today's and still make sense

Nice puzzle, but the structure of the 13D clue is off.

Claude and ChatGPT are NOT "AIS," they are AI "APS." With an obscure crossing word.  I went for the latter, and blew my streak.

[boo]

HALF SLIP
I really enjoyed this one. A fun and entertaining challenge, groaning puns and two minutes under my average. Tip of the cap to the constructors.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: I liked it a lot and for once have no complaints, tortured/torturing/tortuous metaphors, similes, ditties or pontifications.

"My tailor finally picked up the phone!"
"Sounds like a clothes call!"

("Sure seams like it!")

Nice puzzle. Some fun clues and fill. Wrong day. 

As far as the Wordplay column goes, the columnist explains how two of the theme answers work and then says that "we" should figure out the others on our own. Now I didn't need help with that, but I thought one of the main purposes of the column, especially on Thursdays, was to explain all the tricky theme clues for people who need help. 

That's all.

Never before in my long life have I ever learned a piece of trivia and put it to use within 5 minutes. I watched a quiz show immediately prior to starting the crossword. The last question: what is the name given to a cat that has a black coat with white fur on its chest and throat? I had never heard of a TUXEDO cat prior to hearing the quiz answer.

So there is an antelope with two DIKs! Who knew?

AODAI looks so weird! I would never have got it without crosses, and the fact it crossed with two proper names had me scared for a moment. Then I realized the crossword staple of LANAI is not only a traditional porch but also an island, and M(A)E looked better than any other option.

I guess I sort of get the theme, but isn't it a bit... Disjointed? I suppose it's enough that all the themed clues are rebuses  of sorts, even if each of them works differently in detail.

Despite some minor difficulties I solved this in this week's Tuesday time, and in about 55% of my Thursday average. I found the majority of the clues just too straightforward for this time in the week 🤷🏽

Wait, wait...
[Hawaiian cattle call]

MUUMUU
Absolutely delightful. I love what they did to the clues to get the items of clothing. I enjoyed doing this puzzle so much. More, more, more. . . .
So, penultimate letter of the Greek alphabet looks like it’s becoming a regular.

Fun puzzle!

Very clever, enjoyable to do. Thank you to the constructor

I can see why it took years to make today’s theme work. These are hard! Here are my best attempts…

LOVED IT, Thank you.
Finished in 25% less time than average, but it seemed harder than my speed would indicate. Worthy Thursday.

Sometimes you vibe with the constructor, sometimes you don't. Today was one of the former days for me. Breezed through it even though it had its traps.

Can't wait for people to come here complaining that it was too easy, so that I can start feeling stupid again.
I lost at least a minute or two trying to make MINISPLIT work in MINISKIRT’s place. Ah well.
I enjoyed it. And TIL 16A and 48D, which haven't I heard before
wonderful fun Thursday theme is a hoot thanks!
loved the puzzle but got caught up with coif instead of coil and efs is legit as is els. wouldn't have solved without help
Just plain fun.

If this ran on a Wednesday, it would have made it into my early week POY contender file.  

The theme was great fun.  Not only are they all clothing items, but they're featured from head to foot.  Nice attention to detail.

The cluing was pretty straighforward for the non-themers, leaving me feeling let down.

s^c^o^u^n^d^r^e^l

SPIKED HEEL

Fun puzzle!
A lot of easy clueing but entertaining rebuses more than made up for it. Exactamundo! Favorites clues were for miniskirt and cut off tee.

Man, some of these comments. I loved today’s puzzle. Clever theme that I just happened to catch on quickly ( can’t say that often!). I enjoyed the offering very much. Thanks for all the hard work! Hope to see more by you two.

The source  of the Nile is in Nyungwe Forest in Rwanda. Burundi is considered to be in the Nile Basin. 
I’ve been there. Trust me.
Miniskirt doesn't work for evade. There's nothing mini about "evade",it's a 5 letter word, whole,complete and entire.
I don't about you guys/gals but I had fun with this one!  Loved the theme.

Well, the zany outfit itself was fun. 

So was trying to guess the theme answers from their visual and wordplay clue-riddles.

Then there was the richness of uncovering words that strike me as beautiful, such as PIQUE, DIKDIK, EXACTAMUNDO and WREAKS.

Oh, and the fauna-rich collection in the grid warmed my animal-loving heart – LLAMA, MINK, ELKS, DIKDIK, RAY, PURRS, MAA, FERAL, TUXEDO, plus that SKULKing fox and the hidden snake in Portugal’s capital.

The whole vibe in the box was marvelously upbeat, and that on top of everything else I've mentioned made this puzzle so much more than simply filling in the squares.  This was a splendid, splendid experience.  Thank you, Zhou and Mallory!

Really enjoy this kind of lateral thinking puzzle

My contribution: 

Lend a helping ____

HANDOUT

If you are so inclined, might you commenters with pull try to exert some muscle to get us back to the old days where the app could display the puzzle and the comments simultaneously in separate windows?
This is a day when being able to toggle between the puzzle and the column/comments would be extremely convenient. Opening the puzzle in a browser is clunky, and the clues take up half the screen on my droid.
Spoiled-child rant of the day.
Peace.
Obit/Omit vs Bade/Made killed me

Did much better on today's puzzle by this duo than in the past.  But still had to rely on some look-ups to fill in several blank spaces after going through the clues twice.  The usual bad fill in a couple of spots did not help the cause.  Good puzzle, cleverly done.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle was inspired by Mallory Montgomery’s ginger cat (from her profile pic)

a/ everyone knows the sound of a cat, bro
the perfect sound
like a river of jewels like a river
spitting stars like
d/ a piano in a clock
around and around and around
spreading out its
a/ purrs
I did this one while my tuxedo cat purred on my lap!

Do we have a name for weird coincidences between the puzzle and real life?

Today I have a list for the hardware store.  Item #1 - linseed oil.
PDQ!

I was wanting WREAK to be The Word on Wordle, but this is close enough, I guess!

This puzzle probably isn't difficult enough for a Thursday, but the clever clue/entry pairings are such fun that I don't have the heart to complain.  
I did have to back up at 25A; I decided, on the basis of absolutely zero knowledge of Portuguese, that if SAO PAOLO was in Brazil, that LISBAO might be in Portugal.  Close, but no cigar, eh?  
Easily amended by the ever-present OREO!

Loved the fun clues, the Dryden quote, and especially 3D.
Was thinking I would see Mini Mouse or Madonna emerge as a theme until I solved for BACKWARDSCAP.

A puzzle made artificially difficult with trivia and esoterica. Barring the Thursday themes which I always auto-reveal because I can’t be bothered with that ridiculousness, there was some fun cluing even though the answers remained unsatisfying, making the puzzle a negative for me, dawgs. Oh well, at least it wasn’t a rebus.

Tough one for me. I'll leave it at that. Today's puzzle find - A Sunday from July 21, 2013 by Tracy Bennett with the title: "Artful Thinking." Some theme answers: WADEINTHEWATTEAU TISTHECEZANNE HEREWEGAUGIN ONETOOMANET SMOKEANDMIROS QUESERASEURAT WITHFLYNGKAHLOS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/21/2013&g=68&d=A See you tomorrow. ....
I'm picturing a roller-skating hip-hop girl in this outfit.
Fun puzzle. Doffing my cap to constructors.

I liked the puzzle, although I think it would have been even better with a stylish revealer.
"Perhaps something TOPLESS?"
No, not that revealing!!!

And I liked some of the alternate clues that Mallory Montgomery gave in her Constructor Notes.
And I like a good, tricky clue, but...
[   ]
for NO SHOW SOCKS
may be too tricky, even for the best solvers.
Unless I'm missing something,
Because the clue seems to be missing everything!

[Face … … … … plant]




LATE BLOOMER
Very fun!

I love it when this pair come up with a puzzle!

Just one thing: all respect for the new way to clue OREO, but VANILLA, STRAWBERRY and especially CHOCOLATE are the three correct flavours for a milkshake. Oreo is just gonna clog up your straw and you probably deserve it. That is all.

I think this is the second puzzle this week that prints in newspaper format. When I try to print these, the numbers on the left do not show up and the numbers in the square are hard to see. Please stop using this format so frequently.

I alwas solve on the phone app, but did this one on my computer today. Not sure if it was the puzzle being easier than usual or that solving on the computer cuts a substantial amount of time, but I nearly set a record for myself. That was some fun wordplay!

I am always excited to see a Zhou Zhang puzzle. Honestly, this theme was only so-so for me—we’ve seen a lot of similar ones before, done more amusingly, and while I appreciate the clothes tying it together, they didn’t seem particularly inspired (except CUTOFF TEE) and BACKWARDS CAP and STRIPED SOCKS just seemed a little green painty.
On the other hand, I thought the grid and the clueing sparkled and were more challenging than most Thursdays lately. I had KAUAI and KOALA first and since AO DAI and MAE were a Natick for me, that corner took a little work. I’m embarrassed about how long it took me to get MONK since I just finished the video game Kingdom Come part of which takes place in a Benedictine monastery. Overall I just thought there was a lot interesting fill and many clued in unusual and engaging ways. So great job!
For those complaining, I don’t see how MADE or OMIT really fit those clues, if you really think about it, especially OMIT which doesn’t work grammatically or semantically (especially with the question mark). OBIT is very clever there!

Whenever I see 53D, I am reminded of the character of The FERAL Kid, from "The Road Warrior." He had a stainless steel boomerang, which was awesome, but he needed a special glove to catch it safely. The actor who played the Kid, Emil Minty, had a brief acting career and now owns a jewelry store in Sydney.

Yes, I know it was "Mad Max 2," but the first movie was not released in the US, so a sequel, so named, would have been confusing.

Well, ok! #3 on my fastest solves for Thursday. Fun theme, but can't help feeling the cluing for the rest of the puzzle couldn't have been harder, more in line with Thursday's metric. Still fun. Thanks!

Q: How many obscure proper nouns can I squeeze into a grid to make up for my lack of imaginative clueing?

The constructors: Yes!

A very well-constructed puzzle, though the AODAI/LANAI crossing might prove a rough one. The former was new to me, though I did recall the island.

Clever theme, presented expertly. 

Seemed a bit like a Wednesday to me, but took me longer to complete than yesterday's.
Somehow avoided getting clotheslined by this puzzle and finished it under my Thurs average! It felt more like a Wednesday TBH. In fact, I think yesterday’s puzzle was slightly harder than this one.
Well,  I loved it!  Thanks for a truly enjoyable start to my day!

CRYPTIC CLOTHING?WORDPLAY WARDROBE?TYPOGRAPHICAL THREADS?

I scratched my head to try to come up with a raison d'etre for the theme answers -- otherwise known as a revealer.  Couldn't come up with one.  And there isn't one!  So that was the right decision -- indeed the ONLY decision -- Zhou and Mallory.

Clever and at times baffling.  A worthy Thursday.  I love the wordplay and I love the thinking it required me to do.  To the "why?" of the themer conceit, I reply "Why not?"

A 1-letter DNF for me.  I didn't know the Vietnamese garment and I thought that the answer to Chat GPT and Claude might be ATS as in @@.  So I had  AODAT.  Now I see.  They're AIs.  Never thought of that.  And that's because the mantra of my remaining years is going to be: "IF AI LEAVES ME ALONE, I WILL LEAVE AI ALONE."  I intend to go to my grave without even once allowing its intrusive, know-it-all, high-handed and possibly apocalyptic presence into my life.  You heard it here first!

A terrific puzzle, Zhou and Mallory!
Boomer here: I never heard a Beatles cover of Maggie Mae but it’s an absolute classic Rod Stewart hit and one of my first memories of FM radio!
AODAI x LANAI x MAE x AIS x LISBOA x CLIO is a trainwreck of trivia. editors.. anyone home?!?

Loved the puzzle and the theme. However, whatever effect was put on the word "Evade" to make it MINI did not show up on my app. The word was slightly higher up on the bar than others but not visibly smaller.
Loved this one! Thanks to the constructors, editors too.

Fun puzzle, though a bit fast for a Thursday at 25:19.

Funny thing is I had to make several guesses for a few of the answers I didn't know anything about.
Awesome Wednesday puzzle!
Good theme!

Odd combination of some of the most straightforward clues I’ve ever seen in a Thursday puzzle, alongside one of the most obscure words I’ve ever come across (AODAI?). Finished this in record time, and had about 80% done in half of that, so it was eerily breezy for a “tricky” crossword.

This was one of those Thursdays that managed to sit PURRfectly in my trivia wheelhouse and honed crossword skills, so I sailed through, which usually doesn’t happen on the weekends. I loved it! I was especially proud of myself when I confidently filled in ELS, and didn’t balk at timiL. “ITS BAD” made me laugh out loud. 

I also really enjoyed the combo of TUXEDO, FERAL, and PURRS in this one. It instantly reminded me of my special little one-eyed soul cat Baby. He died suddenly from complications of a birth defect last year, at only 8. I didn’t get to say goodbye and it devastated me. (I’ll never forget the puzzle that day had the word “CAT” smack in the middle of it, which I filled in while we sat at the emergency vet.) I still miss him and think of him every day, but for whatever reason I’ve happened to feel a lot of memories and reminders of his presence all over the place this week. This puzzle felt like an extra touch of his paws to my heart 💛 Thank you, Zhou and Mallory!
Three "loves" in a row for Thurday! Hooray!

Puzzle find today - inspired in part by the name of one of today's constructors. Anyway - a Friday from July 27, 1990 by Roger H. Courtney. Will just mention a pair of answers: 59a -"Ousted liberal Chinese official: 1989." ZHAOZIYANG 16A - "Man who replaced 59-across." JIANGZEMIN Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/27/1990&g=59&d=A Hasta la vista. ....
Loved. This. Puzzle.

Enjoyed this one. 

I’ve finally concluded that “obit” is now a standalone noun, and not clued as an abbreviation. I think I’m really late to this party.

I think AODAI will be new to almost all of us.  I didn't have trouble with LANAI, but I did a little with MAE, where the editors could have had pity on us and used an easier clue.  Almost Naticked that one.

When I asked Gemini who wears a backwards cap, cutoff tee, miniskirt and striped socks it said "Pee Wee Herman."  I guess Google is still working on the program.  Anyway, it would have been a lot less green paint-y if they were referencing a particular character or person.  Oh well.

a very fast Thursday for me (15 min faster than usual) but still felt challenging. the northwest corner was the difficult spot that had me tapping around before finally remembering STAN, which helped everything else come together. AODAI, by the way, is a very common and beautiful attire, glad this puzzle introduced many people to it!
Fun puzzle, but not really the level expected on Thursday. I did it in record time.

As a millennial who's trying out crossword puzzles (love the minis and midis), this has to be the hardest most obscure one yet. Got probably 5 clues answers lol.
Are you supposed to read the crossword article first and then solve the puzzle? Maybe crosswords aren't for me after all.
Loved this puzzle, terrific theme that took me quite some time to figure out. Thanks Zhou and Mallory.

the theme's double meanings for the 4 marquee clues are really something to behold - the fill was really easy except for a few beats that made things like a Thursday should be.  AIS is a ghastly term that single or plural is no friend of of true creatives who trust the MIND.
Well, at least it wasn't a rebus.
I gather I'm in the minority knowing the AO DAI, and in a smaller subset having seen it worn in its native land.
Smooth solve, charming theme. My favorite part was actually afterward, reading the description in the column.

I worked with ELTON John at a cafe in Bristol one summer in the 60s, before he shot to fame.  Back then he went by the name Reg. One day a group of women came in for lunch and Reg asked me to cover the table. When I asked him why, he said, “Ugh, the ladies who lunch, barking orders for refills all day, then they all want separate bills, and no tips.”  So I handled the table of women snd they were lovely. When I showed Reg how much they left for tip he couldn’t believe it, and ran down the street after them fuming mad and shouting.  Years later I saw him performing in LA at the Troubadour. After the show he was chatting with a waitress who was showing him how much she had eared that night in tips. Reg immediately ran down Sunset Boulevard chasing after the customers and shouting.  I also remember reading about him after a performance at Madison Square Garden a few years ago, running down 34th street chasing after a taxi cab and shouting when he learned what NYC cabbies earn in tips.  Most recently, I was walking past a hair salon in a strip mall in Germantown Tennessee and there was this man standing in the doorway giving the stylists a hard time about the “exorbitant” tips they earn.  Sure enough, it was ol’ Reg.

I first thought the "Pineapple Isle" in 8D was KAUAI. Given the crosses I already had, that meant the angry spitting animal in 9A was likely a KOALA. I thought, "What? Those adorable little teddy bears spit when they're angry? Can I even imagine them GETTING angry??" It was a disillusioning revelation. Fortunately I eventually  caught my errors and got the happy music. So TIDL that koalas have a nasty side!
Was puzzled by one of Mal M’s comment with the entries that didn’t make the cut: NO SHOW SOCKS;[ ]. Can someone explain, please?

This one was pretty breezy and fun, 'til I filled in  the last square and got that "almost there" message.  Now I've been fly specking on and off for a couple of hours and I can't find a single thing wrong with my fill. I'm about to lose either my streak or my marbles by days' end. Is it "gooier?" "Exactamundo?" Do I need glasses? Hmph.
I've put my rebus radar away for another week.

In the app, 42 across shows Evade slightly higher than the rest of the clues. Does it appear mini-er in the actual paper? 

Just not sure how slightly vertically elevated = mini.

Not sure this will render properly (or at all):

5♠

That clue might work thematically for the answer POWERSUIT.

Best I could muster. My sense from the comments is that few folks realized just how tight and clever today's theme is.

One more late puzzle find. A Friday from March 25, 1994 by Harvey Estes. One sample theme clue and answer: "Fast planes from New York." JFKSSTS And the other theme answers: DRJSBMWS WKRPSDJS NFLSMVPS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/25/1994&g=4&d=D ...

What a delight !! I found the SW nearly impossible but that’s just because I have never seen the relevant TV shows/heard of the relevant people, and apart from that, I really loved it. MINISKIRT is my favourite of the theme clues, and the simplicity of ITSBAD had me chuckling 😂 looking forward to more !

In the top right I started out with camel/llm in for LLAMA/AIS and spent ages staring at the rest that section until I figured those two out.
Still came up short on LANAI/OADAI, that was a brutal cross for me. I'd been hoping one or the other of those answers would turn out to be a fairly common thing that I just hadn't heard of in the context of their clues.
This week is going very poorly.

Thank you Zhou and Mallory for a very fine grid, along with your great constructor notes! So glad you dusted off any early BATTLE SCARS in the co-constructing process and you persevered toward the acceptance WIN! I loved the misdirect clues, especially for 58D and 64A. Looking forward to your next collaboration!😊
Pique won. I actually laughed out loud when I saw that answer. Did anyone else have this issue?

I don’t know if it counts as an alternate solution, but if the first letter of 63A is an M, it seems to fit both clues. Someone ordered to do something is "made" to do it, and if you end a piece, as in get rid of it, you "omit" it.

Clever, I guess,  but not a fan. 

The visual clue for 42 across was a total bust.  How the raised font in the games app is supposed to equate to MINI evades me.  I get how the fits the these, but the clue is a stretch.

lengthW puzzle…. What does that mean? Well WAVELENGTH of course! Meaning if you are not in the constructor(s) brainwave the puzzle is meaningless… 

And waaay too much obscure trivia! 

Hate these types of puzzle but apparently the editors need to clear out the garbage some days…

Rating:
2/10
I feel compelled to rebut all the hateful reviews. I enjoyed this puzzle. Granted, everyone is entitled to their opinion, But I thought it was fun.
@Laura Indeed.   Fun one!
@Barry Ancona MINISKIRT was also a themer! Some people couldn’t see the clue in the minj font, maybe you were among them?

Kate Tani,
I saw it when I solved the puzzle, but it was so simple that when I went to comment I forgot the clue's type size.
Did you not know and fail to guess both of them, or were you just suggesting you thought others might have trouble there?
@Dave K. I guess I have to accept that I am old when everything the Beatles ever wrote, said, sang or did is no longer common knowledge.

@Morn

They don't work because they're wrong.  Your justifications are stretchier than Turkish taffy.
@Morn I did exactly this same thing. Also felt it was plausible.
[End piece?] and OBIT are both nouns. Omit is not a noun. Clue and answer must be the same part of speech.
@Barry Ancona thank you — that is very helpful!

@Morn

I don't think omit would work well for "end piece".  Generally something omitted is a part left out of a larger thing, whether that's omitting a fact from a story, omitting an article from a collection, or whatever. It's left out, it's dropped, but it's not ended.
@Morn if you think a clue having multiple potential answers is a problem, I’m not sure you like crosswords.

@Morn That would be really tricky to find if you were looking for a mistake (or "mistake") in completing the puzzle.

I agree with the other comment, both words are plausible with an M. But I think both fit better with B.

@Morn That's why crosswords have *cross* words! The clues can be ambiguous, and that's part of the challenge. Figuring out which answer fits with the help of the crosses is the game.

You are going to hate Fridays and Saturdays. Those days, you often count yourself lucky when a clue has only three plausible answers! :-)

@Morn

Also, "He ordered me to do it"; "He MADE me do it".  And tonight we will watch the end of a great piece of work by Stephen Colbert, thanks to someone hitting the OMIT button.
@Barry Ancona, "end piece" could be an order from a frustrated piano teacher getting tired of hearing her student murder an etude.  In which case, it's a verb.

Morn: 

The word "omit" means "leave out". I don't see any way that "End piece" can be construed as a clue for "omit".

But also, plenty of clues can have several plausible answers; this is entirely normal. But the intention is that there is only one correct answer that will agree with all the crossing words, and that is almost always the case.

Don H,
Look at the clue again. An order with a question mark?

@Morn I hear you! I confidently filled in CAMEL where LLAMA belongs today - I think the figuring-out-other-plausible-answers is one of my favourite bits of puzzling. It feels too straightforward when it's always the obvious (to me) answer...

@Dan

I think Morn's point is that both 58D and 63D match their clues (in Morn's opinion) with an M in place of the B.  It's not that an individual clue has multiple possible answers; it's that both clues (in Morn's opinion) match OmIT and mADE.

(Personally I agree that BADE and mADE both work for 63A; I disagree about OmIT.)

Usually the crosses decide an ambiguity -- for example if OnIT worked for 58D as well as OBIT, 63A would clarify that because nADE isn't a word, much less one that fits the clue.  Morn was saying it didn't.

@Bob

I posted this in another reply, but in Morn's defense:

I think Morn's point is that both 58D and 63D match their clues (in Morn's opinion) with an M in place of the B.  It's not that an individual clue has multiple possible answers; it's that both clues (in Morn's opinion) match OmIT and mADE.

(Personally I agree that BADE and mADE both work for 63A; I disagree about OmIT.)

Usually the crosses decide an ambiguity -- for example if OnIT worked for 58D as well as OBIT, 63A would clarify that because nADE isn't a word, much less one that fits the clue.  Morn was saying it didn't.
@Morn Thanks for complaining! I had OMIT and couldn't figure out why my puzzle wasn't finishing.

@Mike R You should check out cryptic crosswords! They use similar wordplay to today’s theme. I recommend MinuteCryptic as a way to get started if you’re new.

@Steven M.

My first thought on seeing the theme was "I wonder if the column will call it a rebus, and I wonder how many complaints will ensue", lol.  Because it is a classic rebus, just not a crossword rebus.

@Steven M.

This sort of rebus was the only one I knew for nearly 43 years of my life. I only learned of the NYT crossword concept of a rebus when I started doing these puzzles in 2023. I still don't really understand how the word applies to having multiple letters in a single square 🤷🏽

@Andrzej

Agreed.  It took me a while to get used to this new use of the word "rebus".

No fighting, but what Crosslandia -- at least xwordinfo.com -- calls a rebus puzzle is when the rebus is in the grid. Puzzles like this one with "rebuses" in the clues don't count. And the first NYT XWP rebuses were individual symbols (or numbers) in a square, not the more common "squishes" these days.

656 Rebus puzzles
Some puzzles require you to use symbols or to cram more than one letter into a single box.

@Rahul
I was reading comments to see if anybody else had that same problem. I didn't realize it was a theme clue until I completed the puzzle.

@Rahul The editors really need to double-check the "special effects" in all of the versions before publication. It is very confusing when clues don't show the way the author intended, and even more confusing when people who solved the different versions talk about it. :-)

I just checked the four modes of publication I have available:
* Website: it shows up as a superscript (small letters, shifted up). 
* Printable PDF: small letters, but not as a superscript.
* NYT news app: superscript.
* NYT Games app: "large superscript" (shifted up, but in normal size)

So I guess you were using the Games app.

@Rahul

I had the misfortune of being forced to use the games app today due to travel.  It was only when I got back to my beloved PC that I could see the tiny (uppercase?) font for that clue.
@Bob for what it’s worth Evade showed up as a superscript in the Games app on my iPad. So apparently it differs by platform, too.

@Vic

It was set in smaller type on the iPhone app.

@Vic 
I went back to check, and on my (Samsung) Android , the entire clue is raised, as if each letter was a superscript. Since superscripts (like the "2" indicating a number should be squared) are often - even usually - in a smaller font than the main text, I guess we were supposed to infer that these normal-sized but slightly raised letters should be interpreted as reduced in size (i.e. MINI) characters?

I call that bad app design, which - given the NYT digital crew's history - is no surprise. Still disappointing, but no longer a surprise.  

If it's important to the theme, they should make it work in the app - on *every* platform, not just the one the coder happens to use.

The only obscure or niche answer in this puzzle was AO DAI. Maybe DIK DIK. And they were easily obtained by the crosses.

@Rob

Your alternatives don't really work well. I read "end piece" as a compound noun, and alternate answers could have been something like epilogue or appendix or coda, none of which would work for this puzzle. As for made, unless linked with "to", it's more likely to mean constructed than ordered.
@Rob I also spent a little while thinking ‘made’ made sense but I BADE my instincts and switched to OBIT. It’s more elegantly clued.
We went over this earlier. Crossword clues and answers must be the same parts of speech. [End piece?] and OBIT are nouns, "omit" is a verb.
@Barry Ancona “end piece” could infer an action

Mike,
You're joking, right?

@Barry Ancona

Please end this piece of reasoning.

@Mike
I think you mean imply, but I agree and am not sure why @Barry thought you were joking. OBIT and BADE are much the better answers, but to play devil's advocate, I too first interpreted end as a verb. To give the end to a piece might mean to kill or OmIT it, and mADE made for a slightly tortured but possible answer.

@Jon Ven

They're not talking about places people meet, and I thought that was one example of excellent misdirection. Think circuitry or data graphs or similar.

@JayTee They’re still not necessarily central. Nodes, from the Latin for knots, are just intersections. They could be anywhere. Think of a net (which is the metaphor in play). There are nodes all over it.

@John Nodes on plants are places where new roots may grow, such as where a leaf joins a stem. When propagating new plants from cuttings, you should always include one or more nodes under the soil or the water. From the internet: "A node is the critical point on a plant's stem where leaves, branches, flowers, and aerial roots emerge. It serves as a concentrated hub of growth and cell division. Understanding nodes is essential for plant care, as they dictate where pruning should occur and are required for successful propagation." Here's one of my absolute favorite videos, "The Science of Cuttings": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8UBOMh5WbTo
@Cat Lady Margaret I thought camels are spitters too!
@Cat Lady Margaret I thought camels are spitters too!

I honestly don't see what the big debate about puzzle difficulty is all about.

@coloradoz I believe tuxedos made it into the Gameplay column or its picture this year. When I got this answer I had two reactions: told my tuxedo cat Maya she made it into the NYT xword; and hoped they would put a tuxedo cat in the column again.

@fionatimes I'll never forget the time we brought our rescue cat Bob into the vet's for the first time. We asked the doc if he had any idea what breed of cat he might be, and the vet blurted out, "He's a tuxedo cat!" Twenty-five years later Bob is helping me solve puzzles

Lucek the poodle turned 1 last Saturday. However, lying next to the bed here as I'm browsing the comments he still looks like a puppy 😃 https://imgur.com/a/VtuUzkI Also, please take note of the cultural artefact of slippers: over here we all take off our outside shoes as we enter our homes and change into slippers and house clothes (which is why there are no coins in our sofas - the cash stays in our outside threads). Watching Americans on TV put their shoes on furniture was one of my biggest shocks of the early 1990s 😄

@Andrzej

TIL that the Japanese way is also so in Poland. The mark of a truly civilised society, if you ask me. Never mind shoes on furniture - simply wearing outdoor shoes inside is barbaric/nasty.
@Andrzej I thought those customs were Japanese. I am 1/4 Polish and I never saw my Polish grandmother do these things.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

Well, I'm 100% Polish and in actual Poland. Keeping your shoes on at my grandma's place was one of the biggest no-nos, and that's typical of a majority of Polish households. You should see the slippers section of a typical Polish shoe store - there are dozens upon dozens options for adults and kids, and "kierpce", leather slippers from the mountainous south of the country, are one of the typical Polish souvenirs.

@Andrzej

But, do you have massive slippers that you can fit your shoes inside of?

When I go to a gym (e.g. for pickle ball or weight training), naturally I  change into my indoor exercise shoes. But what if I then want to use the restroom? Of course one cannot use the restroom wearing indoor exercise shoes and then return to the gym! Solution? Super XL clown-size restroom slippers!

Step into them wearing your trainers and then return to the gym without ever having touched the restroom floor.

@Vaer

I read on Wikipedia today that Rod Stewart's choice of a name for his song as also inspired by the same old folk song that the Beatles memorialized.

@Jerry According to Wikipedia, the Beatles covered 25 songs. You can see the list here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_songs_covered_by_the_Beatles Here they are singing Chuck Berry's "Rock and Roll Music." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IRF6nmqcbxo
@Charles respectfully disagree. It's supposed to be a puzzle, not a word filling exercise.

@Charles perhaps you could argue that AODAI and LANAI are quite obscure (I would agree with the former tbh but on its own it’s innocuous). The rest though are fine imo. CLIO is used so regularly it’s basically “crosswordese”, the first five letters of LISBOA are pretty well known, ‘Maggie MAE’ is similarly quite famous, more so when you consider Rod Stewart’s hit with the same pronunciation (although I’m aware they are different songs), and Chat GPT is almost universally known as an A.I. model.

@John CLIO has been used more with other clues though, and its vowel count just makes it one of those words that are perfect for crosswords which is why I consider it “crosswordese”, similar to ARLO, OREO, OGEE, EPEE, etc etc. I’ve only been solving on and off for a couple years and it’s already been burned into my brain. 

LLM and CAMEL is a plausible cross, easy to see why that would’ve slowed you down. I originally had COBRA but realised something was amiss once I got ADD and MAE.
@O thank you, I couldn't figure out how that one was supposed to give us the entry!  I got it from the crosses, but it was still confusing because it didn't look small, just slightly higher.

@Ali J So I just went back to the crossword from March 18, 2025 and upon seeing it remembered being spooked by the answers that day, because not only was CAT right in the middle, but the answer immediately preceding it was HERNIA - which is what ultimately took his life that night. (A tear in his previously-repaired congenital diaphragmatic hernia.)

It was an awful day, but regardless of the life-long heartbreak I’ll carry, the bond between me and that affectionate, feisty, runt of the litter, formerly feral, physical anomaly of a cat was more than worth every bit of it.

@Ali J: I feel for you. I lost my darling Tuxedo Ollie when he threw a clot, suffered an hour long drive to the emergency clinic, and then was gently euthanized while i blubbed uncontrollably out in the waiting room. You never forget them, do you?
@Ali J    ^. .^  /
Amusingly, OBIT, "now" also a word of its own by shortening, has been a standalone noun in English since the 15th Century. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/obit

@Peter Koetters
Not Pippi Longstocking? Except for the cut-off tee and just one striped stocking, I think she's pretty close.

@E G Did you know that the puzzles get harder as the week progresses? In addition, Thursdays always has some extra trickery going on. I suggest concentrating on Monday-Wednesday for a while and you'll start to get the hang of it!

@E G Keep at it! You can read the column before, during or after your solve,  and remember that there's nothing wrong with looking things up. The more crosswords you do, the more you'll see the same entries appear again and again. Five answers will turn into 10, then 15, and so on. It takes practice to be sure, but as you know from the Minis and Midis, it's a great feeling to complete it. Happy solving!

@Laura V
ohhh. I had no idea. That makes a lot of sense. I did know the Sunday puzzle is a behemoth of a challenge but didn't realize other days were gradually harder. Just between us, I only solve them while at work lol and that coincides with the Monday through Friday attempts, although I may be extra busy on Thursdays since I don't recall ever being bogged down by a Thursday puzzle before.

userLocation: Doubt exists only in thought–not action.
commentBody:
@E G
Pattern is M-T-W increasing difficulty.  TH, some tricksy factor in the solve PLUS Wed difficulty. FRI and SAT, increasing  difficulty. SUN, mid-week difficulty PLUS bigger grid and maybe a trick. Any day may have a theme or not.

@E G

There are a lot of tools that can help you complete the puzzles when you're a new player--like me. 

First of all, you can google all kinds of facts and answers.

Then, there are two tools on the puzzle page at the upper right over the Across and Down clues.  There is "Check." You can check if a letter or a word or even the whole puzzle is right. You will get a slanted red line through any wrong letter. That helps.
Also, there is a "Reveal" button there. If you can't figure a letter, word, or the whole puzzle out, you can click "Reveal" and get the right answer--a big help to continue the puzzle.

Also, as you know, you can check the Wordplay column or the comments for more help.

I also do the Wordle puzzle daily, which recalls a lot of five-letter words.

By the way, today's Thursday puzzle was particularly challenging due to some complicated typography tricks in the clues. You can read the comments here to help discombobulate some of that!!

@E G
When you start in on a puzzle and you have filled in the answers you're sure you know (e.g., John Lennon's wife Yoko, and the fill is Ono), browse back and other fills might pop out.  When you come to the end of what you can do on your own, google proper names and see if that helps. You'll be surprised at how much you can fill on your own, but remember, it's your puzzle, and however you solve it is your choice. 

The harder puzzles will take longer, but even when you can't finish them you will have learned a lot, and the satisfaction of getting better is worth the time.

Welcome to Wordplay comments.  We're all delighted to have you with us.

@E G You're not "supposed" to read the article, but you can do whatever you like, so long as it's fun for you. Some people look up answers on the internet; some people look at the answer key. You get to decide how to solve it. Puzzle-solving, like many activities, is tricky at first, and gets easier the more you do it. But you'll only do them if they're fun, so do what's necessary to make it an enjoyable experience. If you're feeling "puzzled", but don't want to give up, you can get answers to your questions here. A good place to start in becoming a better solver is with this article by Deb Amlen: https://www.nytimes.com/article/how-to-solve-a-crossword-puzzle.html
@E G I have done well over 2000 puzzles and today's puzzle eluded me, the north east and southwest in particular. But nevertheless I persist!! Carry on
@Barry Ancona congratulations!

@KK I can't speak for Mal, but my interpretation: [ ] is a blank clue, so it shows nothing for NO SHOW SOCKS. This would be very difficult. Without a clue, you'd have to only rely on crosses. Maybe you could get NO SHOW and make the leap to SOCKS, but still — very tricky!
@KK "no show" socks are so short that they do not "show" when you are wearing showles.
@Dan shoes. Sorry

Sean,
I assumed the same: NO SHOW from the clue and the theme, SOCKS from the crosses (or inferred from the theme if, like STRIPED SOCKS, it was the lowest themer).
@Sean McGowan (and others): “tricky” is being generous! I would have called it “terrible”. Good it didn’t make the cut, then!
@KK all the answers so far have explained the no-show part, but where do we get the "socks" from? There's no 'punch' or 'pow' or other synonym being manipulated...

Pedant,
You're good on those two. I hope you have a B in the 63 square.
